home.title: SnapBack – Ship faster without fearing your last refactor

content.headline: Code Breaks. Snap Back.
content.subheadline: A VS Code extension that automatically snapshots your workspace so you can experiment freely with AI and restore the last working state in one click.
content.founder_story: Local-first, works offline, no account required. Currently in alpha—help shape the product.
content.trust_line: Local-first · Works offline · No account required
content.video_caption: [TODO: 60-second hero video] Real SnapBack session showing Sessions panel → snapshot selection → file restoration → confirmation toast

content.label: The challenge
content.headline: AI moves fast. So do mistakes.
content.body: Copilot, Claude, Gemini—they suggest code at breakneck speed. Some ideas are gold. Others break everything. Git helps if you commit. Local History helps if it's one file. SnapBack saves you mid-thought.

timeline_items.title: AI refactored too much
timeline_items.description: Across 20 files in one go. Which change broke it? Not a commit. Not a save. A single AI suggestion.

timeline_items.title: Tests were passing. Now they're not.
timeline_items.description: Revert to the exact moment before the last suggestion, not the last commit. Get back to green in one click.

timeline_items.title: You don't want to lose the good work.
timeline_items.description: Undo is too coarse. Git history is too fragile. You need a safety net that lets you experiment without fear.

content.closing: SnapBack gives you a way back to the last moment you felt safe. No commits required. Just code and snapshots.

steps.title: Auto snapshots on save
steps.body: Every save creates a local restore point. No setup, no new workflow—keep coding as usual while SnapBack quietly tracks your progress.
steps.screenshot: [TODO: VS Code status bar showing 'SnapBack: 47 snapshots' with green checkmark]

steps.title: Session timeline & diff
steps.body: Scroll through your session and compare snapshots to see exactly what changed. Understand how AI suggestions reshaped your code.
steps.screenshot: [TODO: Detection toast notification showing 'Breaking change detected · file-service.ts']

steps.title: One-click restore
steps.body: Pick any snapshot, hit restore, and your workspace jumps back to that state in seconds. Recover broken sessions without wrestling with Git.

content.label: Session timeline
content.headline: See your coding session as a timeline.
content.description: Every save becomes a snapshot on your session timeline. Scroll back through your AI-assisted session, inspect diffs, and restore any point where things still worked.

content.label: Why developers choose SnapBack
content.headline: Zero latency. No vendor lock-in. Just you and your snapshots.
content.body: SnapBack is built for developers who code alone and teams that ship together. Local-first means instant restores, no network calls, and your data always under your control.

metric_cards.title: Sub-100ms snapshots
metric_cards.description: No editor lag. SnapBack captures your workspace in the background while you keep working.

metric_cards.title: <1s restore time
metric_cards.description: Jump back to any point in seconds. Works on monorepos, microservices, and large projects.

metric_cards.title: 100% local storage
metric_cards.description: Your snapshots live in ~/.snapback. No cloud provider. No subscription required. Just efficient local storage.

content.label: Privacy first
content.headline: Local-first. Private by design.

items.title: Works offline on planes, in tunnels, and on bad Wi-Fi.
items.description: Your snapshots never leave your machine. Instant recovery, zero latency, and no third-party storage—SnapBack works even when you're offline.

items.title: No source code uploads—snapshots live alongside your project.
items.description: Every snapshot is stored locally in ~/.snapback. No cloud services. No tracking. No hidden syncs. Verify it yourself: ls -la ~/.snapback.

items.title: Local-only mode means no login required to get protection.
items.description: You don't need an account to use SnapBack. Start protecting your workspace immediately after install, completely offline.

content.footer: Your code is your responsibility and your asset. SnapBack is transparent about how it handles your work.

content.headline: Install SnapBack for VS Code.
content.body: Start using automatic local snapshots today. Install the extension and take the risk out of AI-assisted coding. We're actively iterating with private alpha teams—your feedback helps shape the roadmap.

content.headline: How SnapBack keeps you safe while you code.
content.body:
SnapBack is a local-first timeline of your development session.
It creates smart snapshots of your workspace at meaningful moments and gives you a fast, precise way to roll back when something goes sideways.

content.headline: Smart snapshots, not noisy backups.
content.body: SnapBack isn’t a glorified “auto-save”. It groups changes around events that actually matter to you.

triggers.title: File saves
triggers.description: Lightweight snapshots keyed to the files you touch most.

triggers.title: Test runs
triggers.description: Before and after your test commands, so you can jump back to “all green”.

triggers.title: Branch switches & pulls
triggers.description: Guardrails when you’re juggling multiple workstreams.

triggers.title: AI-driven edits
triggers.description: Capture the state before a large Copilot / Claude / Gemini edit so you can compare outcomes.

content.headline: Your whole session, as a scrollable timeline.
content.body: SnapBack clusters snapshots into sessions — “Today’s spike”, “API refactor”, “Bug hunt”. Each session keeps your moments in order, so you can scrub back to the one that feels right instead of guessing.
highlights: Semantic labels – “Before auth refactor”, not just “12:03 PM”.
Filters – Show only snapshots linked to test runs or a specific file.
Search – Type “refactor” to jump to moments you deliberately marked.

tabs.label: VS Code
tabs.title: Command palette first
tabs.body: Hit `⌘⇧P` / `Ctrl+Shift+P`, type “SnapBack: Restore Snapshot”, pick the moment, and confirm. Your workspace rolls back in one atomic operation.

tabs.label: CLI
tabs.title: Terminal-friendly
tabs.body: Use `snapback restore --snapshot <id>` to integrate with your own scripts and workflows.

tabs.label: API
tabs.title: Automation hooks
tabs.body: Coming soon: programmatic access for custom tooling, bots, and CI helpers.

content.headline: Under the hood: boring on purpose.
content.body: SnapBack uses a local SQLite datastore to track snapshots and file states. Restores are applied via a cautious engine that checks for conflicts and never touches untracked files.
bullets: Atomic restores – Either the entire operation succeeds, or nothing changes.
Conflict awareness – Warns you if a restore would overwrite newer work.
Audit trail – You can see what changed for every restore operation.

content.headline: What’s coming next
items: Smarter labels powered by local AI context

content.headline: Simple pricing, no tricks.
content.body: Start free as a solo dev. Upgrade when you want to roll it out to your team.

plans.name: Solo
plans.description: For individual developers who want a personal safety net.

plans.name: Pro
plans.description: For people who live in their editor and ship daily.

plans.name: Team
plans.description: For teams that want SnapBack as standard dev tooling.

faqs.question: Will SnapBack upload my source code?
faqs.answer: No. Snapshots stay local. We only use cloud services for auth, updates, and anonymous telemetry you explicitly opt into.

faqs.question: Is this a replacement for git?
faqs.answer: No. SnapBack sits underneath git and your editor. It’s about sessions and workspace state, not commit history.

faqs.question: Does SnapBack slow my editor down?
faqs.answer: We designed it to be invisible until you need it. Snapshotting is lightweight and tuned for active development.

faqs.question: What happens if a restore goes wrong?
faqs.answer: Every restore is tracked and reversible. If something looks off, you can revert the restore itself.

content.headline: Get SnapBack running in under 5 minutes.
content.body: Follow this quick path to install the extension, run your first experiment, and feel the safety net kick in.

steps.title: Install the VS Code extension
steps.body: Search for “SnapBack” in the VS Code marketplace, or click the button below.

steps.title: Connect your account (optional at first)
steps.body: Use GitHub / Google / email to link your account for sync and updates.

steps.title: Run a test refactor
steps.body: Change something a bit scary in a throwaway file. Then use SnapBack to roll back and feel the safety net kick in.

content.headline: Your first restore, step-by-step.

steps.title: Make a change
steps.body: Edit a file, run your tests, or let AI rewrite a chunk of code.

steps.title: Pick the moment
steps.body: Choose the snapshot labeled “Before refactor”. Confirm. Watch your workspace roll back.

content.closing: That’s it. From here, SnapBack quietly watches your back while you code.

content.headline: Make SnapBack part of your muscle memory.

tips.title: Name important snapshots
tips.body: Add little notes like “known-good” to make them easy to find.

tips.title: Restore early, not late
tips.body: If something feels off, jump back quickly instead of thrashing.

tips.title: Use it on spikes
tips.body: Treat experiments as cheap because getting back is cheap.

content.headline: Help shape SnapBack.
content.body: We’re onboarding early users slowly so we can ship the right things. Tell us how you work and we’ll invite you as we expand.
